content.text: ESLint and Prettier are distinct tools with complementary jobs. ESLint analyzes the abstract syntax tree of your code to catch bugs and enforce patterns — it can flag a

missing a dependency, but it does not care about whitespace. Prettier is a code formatter — it reprints your code in a canonical style without touching logic. Used together, they eliminate two entire categories of pull-request comments: "this looks wrong" (ESLint) and "wrong indentation" (Prettier).

attrs.alt: Diagram showing ESLint vs Prettier responsibilities and the VS Code save-cycle order

content.text: When you press Ctrl+S in VS Code, ESLint runs first (auto-fixing what it can), then Prettier reformats the result. ESLint fixes are applied in the document buffer before Prettier sees the file, so the two tools compose cleanly.
already includes ESLint. For a manual install:

npm install -D eslint @eslint/js typescript-eslint \
  eslint-plugin-react-hooks eslint-plugin-react-refresh
ESLint 9 uses the flat config format. Create
Key ESLint Rule Groups for React
Hooks called conditionally or in loops
Missing deps in useEffect / useMemo / useCallback
Prevents escaping the type system with any
Non-component exports that break HMR fast-refresh

rule is one of the most valuable catches ESLint provides for React. It statically analyzes which values your effect callback closes over and requires them to appear in the dependency array. A stale closure bug — where an effect reads an outdated value because the dep was omitted — is otherwise very hard to debug at runtime.
Do not silence exhaustive-deps with eslint-disable.
If ESLint tells you a dep is missing, the correct fix is almost always to add it. The rare cases where you genuinely want to skip a dep (e.g. a stable ref) should use
to make the value stable, not an eslint-disable comment.

— makes the lint script fail even on warnings (not just errors). Useful to keep warning counts from creeping up.
— exits non-zero if any file would change on format, without writing anything. This is the right CI check.
— formats all files in-place. Run this locally, never on CI (CI should only check, not modify files).
Prettier will try to format every file it encounters unless you exclude them. Create

If you need to use legacy config with ESLint 9 (for compatibility with older plugins), set
as an environment variable. Most actively maintained plugins now support flat config.
Prettier has specific JSX defaults that differ from its HTML defaults. Key ones:
(default) — JSX attribute strings use double quotes:
. This is different from JS string literals where
of a JSX element with props goes on its own line for readability.
(default) — many React projects increase this to 100 or 120 because JSX with multiple props quickly overflows 80 characters.
Automated Enforcement with Husky and lint-staged

VS Code's format-on-save only works for developers using VS Code with the extension installed. To enforce formatting and linting for everyone — regardless of editor — use Husky (Git hooks) and lint-staged (run tools only on staged files):

npm install -D husky lint-staged
npx husky init
to run Prettier and ESLint only on the files that are staged for this commit:

automatically formats and lint-fixes only the files being committed. If ESLint finds an error it cannot auto-fix, the commit is blocked and the error is printed, forcing the developer to fix it before the commit goes through.
Occasionally a lint rule is wrong for a specific line. The correct escape hatch is an inline disable comment — not disabling the rule globally:
Every disable comment should have a
note explaining why the rule does not apply in this specific case, so future readers (and you, six months later) understand the intent.
Running ESLint and Prettier in CI
A typical GitHub Actions step for lint and format checks:

# .github/workflows/ci.yml
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: '20', cache: 'npm' }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run lint          # ESLint
      - run: npm run format:check  # Prettier --check
      - run: npm run build         # tsc + vite build
) in CI ensures the exact package versions in
are installed and fails if the lockfile is out of date.
so Node.js parses it as ESM.
Prettier and ESLint formatting rules conflict.
If you see ESLint errors about spacing or quotes that Prettier keeps writing back, you forgot to add

object match file paths relative to the project root, not glob patterns relative to the file's location.
matches all tsx files in the tree.
Any file that is generated by a build step (e.g.,
. Running Prettier on generated JSON can alter its content (e.g., changing number formatting) or cause huge diffs.
TypeScript errors not caught by ESLint.
ESLint with typescript-eslint handles a subset of type checking. It does not replace
Checking What Version of Config Your Project Uses

# Check ESLint version
npx eslint --version

# Print the resolved flat config for a file
npx eslint --print-config src/App.tsx

# Check Prettier config for a file
npx prettier --find-config-path src/App.tsx

By default, typescript-eslint runs in a mode that only analyzes single files without full type information. You can opt into type-aware rules — rules that use the TypeScript compiler API to check cross-file types — by providing
Type-aware rules unlock powerful checks like
(async event handlers that swallow errors), and
(non-string values in template literals). The cost is a slower lint run because ESLint must invoke the TypeScript compiler.
Extending ESLint with Import and Accessibility Rules
Two additional plugins are commonly added to React projects:
— catches wrong import paths, duplicate imports, imports of non-existent modules, and can enforce import ordering.
If you use Tailwind CSS, the official Prettier plugin (
) sorts class names into a consistent order that matches Tailwind's own recommended order. Without it, Tailwind class names accumulate in random order as developers add them, making diffs noisy.
Prettier plugins run alongside the core formatter, so adding this plugin requires no changes to your ESLint config or npm scripts.
Practical Walkthrough: Adding ESLint and Prettier to a Fresh Vite Project
Install Prettier and the conflict resolver:
once to format the whole project, then commit the result as "chore: apply Prettier formatting".
Optionally install Husky + lint-staged for commit-time enforcement.
Add lint + format:check steps to CI.
In a monorepo with multiple React packages, repeating the ESLint and Prettier config in each package is error-prone. The canonical pattern is to publish a shared config package:

packages/
  eslint-config/          # shared ESLint rules
    index.js
    package.json
  prettier-config/        # shared Prettier rules
    index.json
    package.json
  app-1/
    eslint.config.js      # extends @my-org/eslint-config
  app-2/
    eslint.config.js
For Prettier, a shared config is even simpler — just point the
When ESLint reports a rule error you did not expect, start by identifying which config object is activating it:

# See which rules are active and where they come from
npx eslint --print-config src/App.tsx | grep -A2 '"react-hooks/exhaustive-deps"'
For Prettier, if the formatter is not running as expected, check that the file's language ID is recognized:

# Preview what Prettier would write without saving
npx prettier --check src/App.tsx

# See exactly what it would change
npx prettier src/App.tsx | diff src/App.tsx -
Below is a complete, self-contained setup for a Vite + React + TypeScript project with ESLint 9 flat config and Prettier:

# 1. Install all tools
npm install -D \
  eslint @eslint/js typescript-eslint \
  eslint-plugin-react-hooks eslint-plugin-react-refresh \
  prettier eslint-config-prettier
ESLint catches correctness bugs (wrong hook usage, missing deps); Prettier enforces formatting (indentation, quotes, trailing commas). They have distinct, non-overlapping jobs.

rule prevents stale closures — one of the most common React bugs. Avoid suppressing it.

Husky + lint-staged enforces formatting and linting at commit time for all contributors regardless of editor.
